name: Monolith vs Modular Monolith vs Microservices
description: When to use monolith, modular monolith, or microservices — and how to migrate

rows.Modular Monolith: One deployable unit, multiple internal modules. Simple deploy, clear boundaries.
rows.Microservices: Many deployable units. CI/CD complexity multiplies.

rows.Modular Monolith: Multiple teams possible, each owning a module

rows.Microservices: Database per service, no joins across services

rows.Monolith: All or nothing. One bug, all down.
rows.Modular Monolith: Same, but blast radius is contained by module boundaries.
rows.Microservices: Partial degradation, but cascade failures if not careful.

rows.Monolith: Single team, <50 engineers, MVP, unknown future
rows.Modular Monolith: Multiple teams, clear bounded contexts, want microservices-lite
rows.Microservices: Multiple teams, scaling needs differ, regulatory isolation, >50 engineers

recommendation.default: Modular Monolith. It's the sweet spot for most teams. Easy to develop, clear boundaries, can extract services later if needed.
recommendation.use_monolith_when: You have one team, the system is small (<100k LOC), or you're at MVP stage. Don't pay for complexity you don't need.
recommendation.use_microservices_when: You have >5 teams, clear bounded contexts, different scaling needs per service, regulatory requirements force isolation.
recommendation.anti_pattern: Distributed Monolith — microservices that must be deployed together, share a database, have circular dependencies. This is the worst of both worlds.

monolith_to_modular: 1. Identify bounded contexts in the existing code
2. Extract them as internal modules (no deployment change)
3. Enforce module boundaries with tests and architecture fitness functions
4. Now you have a modular monolith
modular_to_microservices: 1. Pick the module with the highest scaling needs or team independence
2. Extract its database (one schema → one database)
3. Extract its API (synchronous calls → HTTP or message queue)
5. Repeat for other modules as needed
